section.title: Peter and Cornelius
section.sectionRationale: As Peter heals Aeneas and raises Dorcas, God prepares him for a far greater work. Through matching visions, a Roman centurion in Caesarea and the apostle Peter are drawn together, God teaches Peter to call no person unclean, and while Peter preaches, the Holy Spirit falls on the Gentiles just as at Pentecost. When the circumcised believers object, Peter recounts it all in Jerusalem, and the church glorifies God that He has granted repentance and life even to the Gentiles. This is the door to the nations opened by God Himself.
section.thesis: God sovereignly opens the gospel to the Gentiles, teaching Peter and the church that He shows no favoritism but pours out His Spirit on all who believe, so that the once-for-all door to the nations is thrown open and the mission of the risen Christ reaches beyond Israel to the whole world.
section.frameNote: Six movements: Peter heals Aeneas and raises Dorcas; Cornelius's vision in Caesarea; Peter's vision of the sheet in Joppa; Peter enters a Gentile house; Peter preaches and the Spirit falls on the Gentiles; and Peter defends the whole event before the church in Jerusalem.

units.label: Peter heals Aeneas and raises Dorcas
units.note: signs that turn many to the Lord

verses.text: As Peter traveled throughout the area, he went to visit the saints in Lydda.
There he found a man named Aeneas who had been paralyzed and bedridden for eight years.
“Aeneas,” Peter said to him, “Jesus Christ heals you! Get up and put away your mat.” Immediately Aeneas got up,
and all who lived in Lydda and Sharon saw him and turned to the Lord.
In Joppa there was a disciple named Tabitha (which is translated as Dorcas), who was always occupied with works of kindness and charity.
At that time, however, she became sick and died, and her body was washed and placed in an upper room.
Since Lydda was near Joppa, the disciples, hearing that Peter was there, sent two men to urge him, “Come to us without delay.”

So Peter got up and went with them. On his arrival, they took him to the upper room. All the widows stood around him, weeping and showing him the tunics and other clothing that Dorcas had made while she was still with them.
Then Peter sent them all out of the room. He knelt down and prayed, and turning toward her body, he said, “Tabitha, get up!” She opened her eyes, and seeing Peter, she sat up.
Peter took her by the hand and helped her up. Then he called the saints and widows and presented her to them alive.
This became known all over Joppa, and many people believed in the Lord.
And Peter stayed for several days in Joppa with a tanner named Simon.

units.label: Cornelius's vision
units.note: a God-fearing centurion is told to send for Peter

verses.text: At Caesarea there was a man named Cornelius, a centurion in what was called the Italian Regiment.
He and all his household were devout and God-fearing. He gave generously to the people and prayed to God regularly.
One day at about the ninth hour, he had a clear vision of an angel of God who came to him and said, “Cornelius!”
Cornelius stared at him in fear and asked, “What is it, Lord?” The angel answered, “Your prayers and gifts to the poor have ascended as a memorial offering before God.
Now send men to Joppa to call for a man named Simon who is called Peter.
He is staying with Simon the tanner, whose house is by the sea.”
When the angel who spoke to him had gone, Cornelius called two of his servants and a devout soldier from among his attendants.
He explained what had happened and sent them to Joppa.

units.label: Peter's vision of the sheet
units.note: do not call unclean what God has made clean

verses.text: The next day at about the sixth hour, as the men were approaching the city on their journey, Peter went up on the roof to pray.
He became hungry and wanted something to eat, but while the meal was being prepared, he fell into a trance.
He saw heaven open and something like a large sheet being let down to earth by its four corners.
It contained all kinds of four-footed animals and reptiles of the earth, as well as birds of the air.
Then a voice said to him: “Get up, Peter, kill and eat!”
“No, Lord!” Peter answered. “I have never eaten anything {{koinos|impure}} or unclean.”
The voice spoke to him a second time: “Do not call anything impure that God has made clean.”
This happened three times, and all at once the sheet was taken back up into heaven.

units.label: Peter enters a Gentile house
units.note: God has shown me to call no man unclean

verses.text: While Peter was puzzling over the meaning of the vision, the men sent by Cornelius found Simon’s house and approached the gate.
They called out to ask if Simon called Peter was staying there.
As Peter continued to reflect on the vision, the Spirit said to him, “Behold, three men are looking for you.
So get up! Go downstairs and accompany them without hesitation, because I have sent them.”
So Peter went down to the men and said, “Here am I, the one you are looking for. Why have you come?”

“Cornelius the centurion has sent us,” they said. “He is a righteous and God-fearing man with a good reputation among the whole Jewish nation. A holy angel instructed him to request your presence in his home so he could hear a message from you.”
So Peter invited them in as his guests. And the next day he got ready and went with them, accompanied by some of the brothers from Joppa.
The following day he arrived in Caesarea, where Cornelius was expecting them and had called together his relatives and close friends.
As Peter was about to enter, Cornelius met him and fell at his feet to worship him.
But Peter helped him up. “Stand up,” he said, “I am only a man myself.”
As Peter talked with him, he went inside and found many people gathered together.
He said to them, “You know how unlawful it is for a Jew to associate with a foreigner or visit him. But God has shown me that I should not call any man impure or unclean.
So when I was invited, I came without objection. I ask, then, why have you sent for me?”
Cornelius answered: “Four days ago I was in my house praying at this, the ninth hour. Suddenly a man in radiant clothing stood before me
and said, ‘Cornelius, your prayer has been heard, and your gifts to the poor have been remembered before God.
Therefore send to Joppa for Simon, who is called Peter. He is a guest in the home of Simon the tanner, by the sea.’
So I sent for you immediately, and you were kind enough to come. Now then, we are all here in the presence of God to listen to everything the Lord has instructed you to tell us.”

units.label: Peter preaches and the Spirit falls on the Gentiles
units.note: God shows no favoritism, and the gift is poured out on the nations

verses.text: Then Peter began to speak: “I now truly understand that God does not show {{prosopolemptes|favoritism}},
but welcomes those from every nation who fear Him and do what is right.
He has sent this message to the people of Israel, proclaiming the gospel of peace through Jesus Christ, who is Lord of all.
You yourselves know what has happened throughout Judea, beginning in Galilee with the baptism that John proclaimed:
how God anointed Jesus of Nazareth with the Holy Spirit and with power, and how Jesus went around doing good and healing all who were oppressed by the devil, because God was with Him.
We are witnesses of all that He did, both in the land of the Jews and in Jerusalem. And although they put Him to death by hanging Him on a tree,
God raised Him up on the third day and caused Him to be seen,
not by all the people, but by the witnesses God had chosen beforehand, by us who ate and drank with Him after He rose from the dead.
And He commanded us to preach to the people and to testify that He is the One appointed by God to judge the living and the dead.
All the prophets testify about Him that everyone who believes in Him receives forgiveness of sins through His name.”
While Peter was still speaking these words, the Holy Spirit fell upon all who heard his message.
All the circumcised believers who had accompanied Peter were astounded that the gift of the Holy Spirit had been {{ekcheo|poured out}} even on the {{ethnos|Gentiles}}.
For they heard them speaking in tongues and exalting God. Then Peter said,
“Can anyone withhold the water to baptize these people? They have received the Holy Spirit just as we have!”
So he ordered that they be baptized in the name of Jesus Christ. Then they asked him to stay for a few days.

units.label: Peter defends it in Jerusalem
units.note: God has granted even the Gentiles repentance unto life

verses.text: The apostles and brothers throughout Judea soon heard that the Gentiles also had received the word of God.
So when Peter went up to Jerusalem, the circumcised believers took issue with him
and said, “You visited uncircumcised men and ate with them.”
But Peter began and explained to them the whole sequence of events:
“I was in the city of Joppa praying, and in a trance I saw a vision of something like a large sheet being let down from heaven by its four corners, and it came right down to me.
I looked at it closely and saw four-footed animals of the earth, wild beasts, reptiles, and birds of the air.
Then I heard a voice saying to me, ‘Get up, Peter, kill and eat.’
‘No, Lord,’ I said, ‘for nothing impure or unclean has ever entered my mouth.’
But the voice spoke from heaven a second time, ‘Do not call anything impure that God has made clean.’
This happened three times, and everything was drawn back up into heaven.
Just then three men sent to me from Caesarea stopped at the house where I was staying.
The Spirit told me to accompany them without hesitation. These six brothers also went with me, and we entered the man’s home.
He told us how he had seen an angel standing in his house and saying, ‘Send to Joppa for Simon who is called Peter.
He will convey to you a message by which you and all your household will be saved.’
As I began to speak, the Holy Spirit fell upon them, just as He had fallen upon us at the beginning.
Then I remembered the word of the Lord, how He said, ‘John baptized with water, but you will be baptized with the Holy Spirit.’
So if God gave them the same gift He gave us who believed in the Lord Jesus Christ, who was I to hinder the work of God?”
When they heard this, they had no further objections, and they glorified God, saying, “So then, God has granted even the Gentiles repentance unto life.”

koinos.gloss: Common, ordinary, and so ritually impure or defiled. Peter's refusal, I have never eaten anything common, reflects the food laws that fenced Israel off from the nations. God's word, do not call common what I have made clean, reaches past food to people, tearing down the wall between Jew and Gentile.

prosopolemptes.gloss: One who shows partiality or accepts persons by outward appearance. Peter confesses that God is not such a One; He plays no favorites among the nations but welcomes all who fear Him and turn to Christ, the truth that unlocks the Gentile mission.

ekcheo.gloss: To pour out, to shed abroad. The same word describes the outpouring of the Spirit at Pentecost, now poured out on Gentiles, showing that God has given them the identical gift and made no second-class members in His church.

ethnos.gloss: A nation or people; in the plural, the Gentiles, the nations outside Israel. That the gift falls on the ethne is the astonishment of the circumcised believers and the hinge of the whole episode, the door of the gospel thrown open to the world.

basic.context: Peter is on a healing tour, restoring Aeneas and raising Dorcas, when God sets up a much larger work. A devout Roman officer named Cornelius is told by an angel to send for Peter. Meanwhile Peter sees a vision of a sheet full of unclean animals and hears God say not to call unclean what He has made clean. The two men meet, Peter preaches Christ, and the Holy Spirit falls on the Gentiles as they listen. Back in Jerusalem some object that Peter ate with the uncircumcised, but when he tells the story they praise God. The key questions are: what does the sheet vision really mean, why does the Spirit fall as it does, and is this a pattern or a one-time opening of the door.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the whole long section as one connected argument that God, not Peter, opens the gospel to the Gentiles. The sheet vision is about people, not diet, as Peter himself explains in verse 28. The falling of the Spirit before baptism is God's public seal that Gentiles are received on the same terms as Jews. The Jerusalem debate shows the church learning to bow to what God has plainly done.
basic.originalLanguages: Peter will not eat what is koinos, common or unclean, until God overturns the whole category. He then confesses that God is no prosopolemptes, no shower of favoritism. When the Spirit is ekcheo, poured out, on the ethne, the Gentiles, the same gift given at Pentecost, the matter is settled. The words carry the story from the wall of separation to its fall.
basic.typology: Peter's raising of Dorcas echoes Jesus raising the dead and points to the resurrection life the gospel brings. The clean and unclean animals fulfil and then retire the food laws that had pictured Israel's separateness. This Gentile Pentecost answers the Babel scattering, gathering the nations by the one Spirit.
basic.christ: Peter's sermon is the centre: Jesus of Nazareth anointed with the Spirit, doing good, crucified on a tree, raised on the third day, appointed judge, and the one through whose name everyone who believes receives forgiveness. It is while this Christ is preached that the Spirit falls. The door to the nations opens only because Christ is Lord of all.
basic.god: God directs every step: the visions, the timing, the command to go, and the outpouring of the Spirit. He shows no favoritism, welcomes those from every nation, and grants even the Gentiles repentance unto life. The whole episode magnifies God's initiative; the church can only recognise and rejoice in what He has done.
basic.crossReferences: Acts 2 is the Pentecost this outpouring matches. Acts 15 settles the question this event raised, that Gentiles are saved by grace apart from the law. Galatians 2 recalls the ongoing struggle over eating with Gentiles. Ephesians 2 celebrates the wall of hostility torn down.
basic.oneStory: This is the door to the nations opened by God's own hand. The promise that in Abraham all peoples would be blessed reaches a decisive fulfilment as the Spirit falls on a Gentile household. From here the gospel is unmistakably for the world, and the church can never again treat any nation as unclean.

context.literary: Luke gives this episode more space than almost any other in Acts, telling the sheet vision and its outcome twice, once as narrative and once on Peter's lips in Jerusalem. The healings of Aeneas and Dorcas prepare the way by moving Peter toward the coastal, Gentile-facing city of Caesarea. The repetition signals that Luke sees this as a turning point of the whole book.
context.historical: Cornelius was a centurion of the Italian Cohort, a God-fearer who worshipped Israel's God without becoming a full proselyte. Jewish custom strongly discouraged entering a Gentile home or sharing a table, so Peter's actions were socially and religiously explosive. Caesarea was the Roman provincial capital, a fitting stage for the gospel's turn to the nations.
context.canonical: The Cornelius account is the narrative counterpart to the doctrine of Ephesians and Romans that Jew and Gentile are one in Christ. It sets up the Jerusalem Council of Acts 15 and undergirds Paul's whole mission. It also anchors the church's conviction that the Old Testament food laws were fulfilled and set aside in Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: Luke narrates at length how God Himself, by vision and by the outpoured Spirit, opened the gospel to the Gentiles, to show that their inclusion is God's doing and not human innovation, and to teach the church to receive all believers without partiality.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The visions, the exact timing, and the Spirit falling before baptism describe how God unveiled a once-hidden mystery at a decisive moment. What is prescriptive and abiding is that God shows no favoritism, that the gospel is for every nation, that cleanness is now defined by Christ, and that salvation comes to all who believe in His name.

debates.question: Is the falling of the Spirit on Cornelius's household a repeatable pattern for later believers, or the once-for-all opening of the door to the Gentiles?

views.label: A once-for-all, representative opening of the door to the nations
views.case: The strongest case reads this as a unique, epoch-making event, a Gentile Pentecost. God gives visible, audible proof, the Spirit falling and tongues, precisely because the inclusion of the Gentiles was being questioned; Peter appeals to it in Acts 11 and 15 as the decisive sign that God had accepted the Gentiles. The visible manifestations serve the once-for-all breakthrough, not a rule that every believer must later receive the Spirit in the same way, since the New Testament normally ties the gift of the Spirit to faith in Christ.

views.label: A normative pattern of Spirit-reception to be expected by all
views.case: Some take the tongues and the outpouring as a model each believer should seek as a distinct experience. This rightly honours the reality and power of the Spirit's coming, but it generalises an event Luke frames as a singular vindication of Gentile inclusion, and it sits awkwardly with texts where the Spirit is received simply by believing the gospel.

debates.bearing: Reading it as the door to the nations opened once for all protects both the unity of Jew and Gentile in one Spirit and the truth that the Spirit is given to all who trust Christ, while still marveling at the sovereign way God announced the breakthrough.

study.originalLanguages: Peter's whole world is ordered by what is koinos, common or unclean, until the voice from heaven abolishes the category, do not call common what God has cleansed. He then declares that God is no prosopolemptes, no respecter of persons or shower of favoritism. The proof arrives when the Spirit is ekcheo, poured out, on the ethne, the Gentiles, the very outpouring language of Pentecost. What was fenced off is now welcomed in, by God's own act.

typology.fulfillment: A sign of the resurrection life Christ gives, echoing Jesus' own raising of the dead and pointing to the final resurrection.

typology.type: The clean and unclean animals in the sheet
typology.fulfillment: The food laws that pictured Israel's separateness are fulfilled and set aside in Christ, who makes all who believe clean.

typology.type: The Spirit poured out on the Gentiles
typology.fulfillment: A reversal of Babel's scattering, as God gathers the nations into one people by one Spirit through the risen Christ.

study.christ: Peter's sermon puts Christ at the centre of the whole event. Jesus of Nazareth was anointed with the Spirit and power, went about doing good, was crucified on a tree, raised on the third day, and appointed by God to judge the living and the dead, and through His name everyone who believes receives forgiveness. The Spirit falls while this Christ is being preached, for it is union with Him, not descent from Abraham, that makes a person clean and brings them into the people of God.
study.god: The episode is a display of God's sovereign initiative and impartial grace. He orchestrates the visions, sends the Spirit, and grants even the Gentiles repentance unto life. He shows no favoritism, welcoming those from every nation who fear Him. The church does not decide to include the Gentiles; it discovers that God already has, and can only glorify Him for it.

crossReferences.note: The Pentecost outpouring this Gentile reception deliberately mirrors, the Spirit poured out on all flesh.
Peter appeals to the Cornelius event at the Jerusalem Council to prove Gentiles are saved by grace, not the law.
Christ has broken down the dividing wall of hostility, making Jew and Gentile one, the doctrine enacted here.
The later conflict over eating with Gentiles shows how hard-won and important this lesson was.

study.oneStory: Here God throws open the door to the nations with His own hand. The ancient promise that all peoples would be blessed in Abraham reaches a decisive turn as the Spirit falls on a Roman household and the church confesses that God has granted even the Gentiles life. From this point the gospel is beyond dispute a message for the whole world, carried by the risen Christ toward the ends of the earth.

sermon.title: Converted by the Spirit (Acts 10)
sermon.why: Keller uses Cornelius as a case study to show that conversion comes through God's initiative, the challenge to mere religion, the transforming work of the Spirit, and the words of the gospel, drawing out exactly how God opens the door to the Gentiles in this passage.

quiz.q: What did Peter's vision of the sheet ultimately mean?

options.text: That God was declaring people clean, so Peter should not call any person impure or unclean.
options.note: Peter himself gives this interpretation in verse 28; the vision was about people, not merely food.

options.text: That the Old Testament food laws were no longer binding on believers.
options.note: True as a real implication, but the deeper point Peter draws is the acceptance of Gentile people.

options.text: That Peter should be less strict about his personal diet.
options.note: This reduces a world-changing revelation to a private matter of eating.

options.text: That God was testing whether Peter would disobey a clear command.
options.note: It was not a test to trap Peter but a revelation opening the gospel to the nations.

quiz.q: What did Peter come to understand about God through this event?

options.text: That God shows no favoritism but welcomes those from every nation who fear Him.
options.note: Peter states this directly as he begins to preach in verse 34.

options.text: That God accepts anyone who is sincere and does good works.
options.note: This distorts the point into salvation by sincerity; Peter goes on to preach faith in Christ for forgiveness.

options.text: That God had always intended to include the Gentiles in His plan.
options.note: True and taught elsewhere, but the immediate lesson is God's impartiality now revealed in action.

options.text: That God favors the Gentiles over Israel from now on.
options.note: God shows no partiality either way; He welcomes all who believe, not one nation over another.

quiz.q: How is the Spirit falling on Cornelius's household best understood?

options.text: As the once-for-all opening of the door to the Gentiles, publicly proving God had accepted them.
options.note: Peter appeals to it in Acts 11 and 15 as the decisive sign of Gentile inclusion.

options.text: As a pattern every believer must repeat to receive the Spirit in the same way.
options.note: This generalises a singular breakthrough that Luke frames as vindicating Gentile inclusion.

options.text: As God honouring Cornelius's sincere prayers and generous gifts.
options.note: His prayers were remembered, but the outpouring proves inclusion by grace through faith, not a reward for works.

options.text: As proof that the Gentiles did not really need to hear the gospel preached.
options.note: The Spirit fell precisely while Peter preached Christ; the word was essential, not bypassed.

quiz.q: Why did the believers in Jerusalem object to Peter?

options.text: Because he had visited uncircumcised men and eaten with them.
options.note: Verse 3 records exactly this charge against Peter.

options.text: Because they doubted whether Gentiles could really be saved at all.
options.note: That doubt lay underneath, but the stated objection was table fellowship with the uncircumcised.

options.text: Because Peter had baptized people without the church's permission.
options.note: The complaint was about associating and eating with Gentiles, not about unauthorized baptism.

options.text: Because Peter had abandoned faith in Jesus.
options.note: No one questioned Peter's faith; they questioned his fellowship with Gentiles.

quiz.q: How did the Jerusalem church respond once Peter explained everything?

options.text: They glorified God, saying He had granted even the Gentiles repentance unto life.
options.note: Verse 18 records their praise and this confession.

options.text: They accepted Peter's account and dropped their objections.
options.note: True, but it stops short of the fuller response, which was active praise to God for including the Gentiles.

options.text: They agreed on the condition that the Gentiles first be circumcised.
options.note: No such condition is set here; that debate comes later and is settled by grace in Acts 15.

options.text: They rejected Peter's report and remained opposed.
options.note: The opposite happened; they had no further objections and praised God.

questions: God told Peter to stop calling unclean what He had made clean. Are there kinds of people you quietly treat as beyond the gospel, and how does this passage confront that?
Peter had to cross deep social and religious barriers to enter Cornelius's house. What barriers make it hard for you to share Christ with people unlike yourself?
Cornelius gathered his whole household to hear the message. Who are the people in your circle you could invite to hear the gospel together?
The Jerusalem church learned to bow to what God had plainly done, even when it upset their assumptions. Where might God be asking you to rethink a settled assumption in light of His word and work?
The Spirit fell as the good news of Christ crucified and risen was preached. How does it encourage you that God works through the simple proclamation of Jesus?

translationNotes.note: The word rendered impure is literally common, the ordinary term for what was ritually defiled or off-limits under the food laws; the same word recurs in verses 15 and 28 and in Peter's retelling, holding food and people together in one lesson.

The BSB prints an em dash after caused Him to be seen, running into verse 41. To avoid dashes this study ends verse 40 with a comma; the sense is unchanged, that the risen Christ was seen not by all but by the chosen witnesses.

Peter recalls the Lord's word from Acts 1:5, John baptized with water, but you will be baptized with the Holy Spirit; the BSB footnote gives an alternative rendering, in a few days you will be baptized in the Holy Spirit.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Acts 9, 10, and 11 (movement spans 9:32-11:18). Footnote markers and line-break objects were skipped when building verse text. The single em dash in Acts 10:40 was converted to a comma, noted in translationNotes. No other em or en dashes appear. Sermon verified this session: Tim Keller, Converted by the Spirit, Acts 10, on gospelinlife.com.
